questions.question_text: A seller owns a duplex and receives a written offer from a buyer. What must the seller deliver to the buyer?
distractors: A professional home inspection report
A preliminary title insurance commitment
A municipal certificate of occupancy
questions.explanation: Under ORS 105.462-105.490, sellers of residential properties with 1-4 units must deliver a completed Seller's Property Disclosure Statement to each buyer who makes a written offer.

questions.question_text: An Oregon seller discloses only issues she has personally observed over 10 years of ownership. Has she met her disclosure obligation?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, disclosure is based on actual knowledge
distractors: No, she must hire a professional inspector
No, she must research public records
No, she must disclose all possible defects
questions.explanation: Oregon's disclosure requirement is based on the seller's actual knowledge. The seller is not required to conduct investigations, hire inspectors, or research public records to complete the disclosure form.

questions.question_text: Which property type triggers Oregon's seller disclosure requirement?
questions.correct_answer: A manufactured dwelling on seller-owned land
distractors: A 10-unit residential apartment building
A vacant commercially-zoned lot
An industrial warehouse property
questions.explanation: Oregon's disclosure law applies to residential properties of 1-4 units, condominiums, timeshares, and manufactured dwellings situated on land owned by the seller. Commercial and larger multifamily properties are not covered.

questions.question_text: A condominium seller receives three simultaneous written offers. How many disclosure statements must the seller provide?
questions.correct_answer: One to each buyer who made an offer
distractors: Only one to the accepted buyer
One shared among all three buyers
None until an offer is accepted
questions.explanation: The seller must deliver a completed disclosure statement to each buyer who makes a written offer, not just the buyer whose offer is ultimately accepted. Three written offers means three disclosures are required.

questions.question_text: What form must Oregon's Seller's Property Disclosure Statement follow?
questions.correct_answer: It must substantially follow the statutory form
distractors: Any format chosen by the seller
The listing broker's preferred template
A form approved by the local MLS
questions.explanation: Under ORS 105.464, the Seller's Property Disclosure Statement must substantially follow the statutory form prescribed by Oregon law. Custom or broker-created forms that deviate significantly from the statutory format do not satisfy the requirement.

questions.question_text: A seller knows the property has a history of basement flooding during heavy rains. Under which disclosure category does this fall?
distractors: Structural and mechanical systems
Neighborhood conditions
Title and boundary issues
questions.explanation: Oregon's disclosure statement includes a natural hazards section covering flooding, landslides, and similar environmental risks. A history of basement flooding during rain events is a natural hazard disclosure item.

questions.question_text: A property is transferred through a bankruptcy proceeding. Must the transferor provide Oregon's seller disclosure?
distractors: Yes, all transfers require disclosure
Yes, unless the court orders otherwise
Only if the buyer requests it
questions.explanation: Oregon exempts court-ordered transfers from the disclosure requirement. This includes foreclosures, bankruptcy proceedings, and probate transfers, since the transferor may lack personal knowledge of the property's condition.

questions.question_text: Which environmental hazard is specifically included in Oregon's required disclosure topics?
distractors: Proximity to cell towers
Distance to nearest hospital
Local crime statistics
questions.explanation: Oregon's disclosure form covers environmental hazards including lead-based paint, asbestos, radon, and mold. Proximity to cell towers, hospitals, and crime data are not required disclosure items under the statute.

questions.question_text: Two co-owners of a home are dividing the property as part of a dissolution. Must they provide a disclosure statement for the transfer?
questions.correct_answer: No, transfers between co-owners are exempt
distractors: Yes, both parties must complete disclosures
Yes, unless they sign a mutual waiver
Only the transferring co-owner must disclose
questions.explanation: Transfers between co-owners, spouses, or domestic partners are exempt from Oregon's seller disclosure requirements. A property division between co-owners falls squarely within this exemption.

questions.question_text: A builder sells a newly constructed home with a full builder warranty. Which statement about disclosure is correct?
questions.correct_answer: New construction with builder warranty is exempt
distractors: The builder must still complete the form
Only the foundation section must be disclosed
Disclosure is required after the warranty expires
questions.explanation: New construction sold with a builder's warranty is exempt from Oregon's seller disclosure requirements. The builder warranty serves as an alternative form of buyer protection for newly built properties.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks the listing agent whether anyone died in the home. What is the agent legally required to disclose?
questions.correct_answer: Nothing; death on property need not be disclosed
distractors: Only deaths within the past three years
Only homicides, not natural deaths
All deaths the agent knows about
questions.explanation: Under Oregon law, sellers and agents are not required to disclose that a death occurred on the property, whether by homicide, suicide, or natural causes. These are classified as stigmatized property issues, not material defects.

questions.question_text: Which fact about a property's history is classified as a stigmatized property issue in Oregon?
questions.correct_answer: A former occupant was diagnosed with HIV
distractors: The property contains asbestos insulation
The septic system has failed twice
The roof was replaced after storm damage
questions.explanation: Oregon law specifically classifies a former occupant's HIV/AIDS status as a stigmatized property issue. Sellers and agents are not required to disclose this information. Asbestos, septic failures, and roof damage are material conditions that must be disclosed.

questions.question_text: A seller refuses to provide a disclosure statement. The buyer proceeds anyway but later wants to cancel. What is the buyer's right?
questions.correct_answer: The buyer may revoke at any time before closing
distractors: The buyer has 5 days to revoke
The buyer cannot revoke after making an offer
The buyer must request a court order to cancel
questions.explanation: When a seller refuses to provide a property disclosure statement, the buyer retains the right to revoke the offer or contract at any time before closing. This open-ended revocation right protects buyers who proceed without receiving required disclosures.

questions.question_text: A buyer receives the seller's disclosure on Monday and discovers a previously unknown landslide risk. By when must the buyer act to revoke?
questions.correct_answer: Within 5 days of receiving the disclosure
distractors: Within 3 days of receiving the disclosure
Within 10 days of receiving the disclosure
Before the next business day
questions.explanation: When a buyer receives a disclosure revealing material information not previously known, the buyer may revoke within 5 days of receiving that disclosure. This 5-day window begins when the disclosure is actually delivered to the buyer.

questions.question_text: A suicide occurred in a home two years ago. The seller's agent knows this fact. How should the agent handle a buyer's inquiry about the property's history?
questions.correct_answer: The agent has no duty to volunteer this fact
distractors: The agent must disclose it if asked directly
The agent must always disclose violent deaths
The agent must note it on the disclosure form
questions.explanation: Oregon law protects sellers and agents from having to disclose deaths on a property, including suicides. This protection applies regardless of how recently the death occurred and whether the agent is asked directly. Deaths are stigmatized property issues, not material defects.
